section.title: The LORD's Answer and the Five Woes
section.sectionRationale: This is the heart of the book and the answer to both of the prophet's complaints. God tells Habakkuk to write the vision and wait for it, gives the line that will shape the whole New Testament gospel, the righteous will live by faith, and then pronounces five woes on the proud oppressor, promising that the earth will be filled with the knowledge of His glory and that all the earth must fall silent before Him.
section.thesis: God answers that the vision has an appointed time, that the proud will collapse under their own arrogance while the righteous will live by faith, and that Babylon and every proud power stands under woe, for the earth will be filled with the knowledge of the glory of the LORD.
section.frameNote: Four movements: write the vision and wait for the appointed time; the contrast of the proud and the righteous who lives by faith; the five woes against greed, injustice, bloodshed, cruelty, and idolatry; and the closing call, the LORD is in His holy temple, let all the earth be silent.

units.label: Write the vision, wait for it

verses.text: Then the LORD answered me: “Write down this {{chazon|vision}} and clearly inscribe it on tablets, so that a herald may run with it.
For the vision awaits an appointed time; it testifies of the end and does not lie. Though it lingers, wait for it, since it will surely come and will not delay.

units.label: The proud and the righteous
units.note: the righteous will live by faith

verses.text: Look at the proud one; his soul is not upright, but the righteous will live by {{emunah|faith}}.

and wealth indeed betrays him. He is an arrogant man never at rest. He enlarges his appetite like Sheol, and like Death, he is never satisfied. He gathers all the nations to himself and collects all the peoples as his own.

Will not all of these take up a taunt against him, speaking with mockery and derision: ‘{{hoy|Woe}} to him who amasses what is not his and makes himself rich with many loans! How long will this go on?’
Will not your creditors suddenly arise and those who disturb you awaken? Then you will become their prey.
Because you have plundered many nations, the remnant of the people will plunder you, because of your bloodshed against man and your violence against the land, the city, and all their dwellers.
Woe to him who builds his house by unjust gain, to place his nest on high and escape the hand of disaster!
You have plotted shame for your house by cutting off many peoples and forfeiting your life.
For the stones will cry out from the wall, and the rafters will echo it from the woodwork.
Woe to him who builds a city with bloodshed and establishes a town by iniquity!
Is it not indeed from the LORD of Hosts that the labor of the people only feeds the fire, and the nations weary themselves in vain?
For the earth will be filled with the knowledge of the {{kavod|glory}} of the LORD as the waters cover the sea.
Woe to him who gives drink to his neighbors, pouring it from the wineskin until they are drunk, in order to gaze at their nakedness!
You will be filled with shame instead of glory. You too must drink and expose your uncircumcision! The cup in the LORD’s right hand will come around to you, and utter disgrace will cover your glory.

For your violence against Lebanon will overwhelm you, and the destruction of animals will terrify you, because of your bloodshed against men and your violence against the land, the city, and all their dwellers.
What use is an {{elil|idol}}, that a craftsman should carve it, or an image, a teacher of lies? For its maker trusts in his own creation; he makes idols that cannot speak.
Woe to him who says to wood, ‘Awake!’ or to silent stone, ‘Arise!’ Can it give guidance? Behold, it is overlaid with gold and silver, yet there is no breath in it at all.”

units.label: Silence before the LORD
units.note: He is in His holy temple

verses.text: But the LORD is in His holy temple; let all the earth be silent before Him.

chazon.gloss: A vision or revelation, a word seen and received from God. Habakkuk is told to write the chazon plainly on tablets so a herald can run with it. It is a sure prophetic word with an appointed time, meant to be published and trusted while God's people wait for it to come to pass.

emunah.gloss: Faithfulness, steadfastness, and so faith: firm trust that holds fast to God. The word carries both leaning on God and being loyal to Him. In 2:4 the righteous live by emunah, trusting God's promise while they wait; the New Testament draws out that this trust, not human achievement, is how a person is justified before God.

hoy.gloss: Woe, an anguished cry of warning and lament, like alas or ah. It opens each of the five taunts against the oppressor. A hoy is a funeral-like announcement of coming judgment: the proud plunderer is as good as dead, and creation itself will testify against him.

kavod.gloss: Glory, weight, honor, the visible splendor of God's presence. The promise that the earth will be filled with the knowledge of the glory of the LORD is the high point of the chapter: every proud empire will pass, but God's glory will flood the whole world as surely as water fills the sea.

elil.gloss: An idol, a worthless thing, a nothing. The word is deliberately dismissive: idols are empty, powerless, and mute. The last woe exposes the folly of trusting what human hands have made, a teacher of lies with no breath in it, in contrast to the living LORD in His holy temple.

basic.context: This is God's full answer to Habakkuk. First, God tells him to write the vision clearly so it can be passed on, and to wait for it, because it will certainly come at the right time. Then comes the pivotal line: the proud will not endure, but the righteous will live by faith. After that, God pronounces five woes on the arrogant oppressor for greed, dishonest gain, bloodshed, cruelty, and idolatry, promising that one day the whole earth will be filled with the knowledge of His glory, and calling all the earth to fall silent before Him.
basic.hermeneutics: Verse 4 is the hinge of the whole book and one of the most important sentences in the Bible. In its setting it means that while the proud Babylonian trusts his own strength, God's people are to survive the crisis by trusting God and His promise. The New Testament takes up this same principle and shows its full depth: a person is made right with God not by works but by faith. The five woes are not random; they expose the way the proud life destroys itself and others.
basic.originalLanguages: God tells Habakkuk to write the chazon, the vision, a sure word with an appointed time. The proud man's soul is not upright, but the righteous will live by emunah, a word meaning both faith and faithfulness, a trust that holds on to God. Each judgment opens with hoy, woe, a funeral cry. The chapter peaks on kavod, the glory of the LORD, filling the earth, and ends by exposing the elil, the worthless idol, with no breath in it.
basic.typology: The vision that waits for an appointed time and will surely come points to the coming of Christ and, beyond, to His return; Hebrews applies the words to the one who is coming and will not delay. The cup of God's wrath passed to the oppressor is the cup Jesus drinks in the place of His people. And the earth filled with God's glory anticipates the new creation.
basic.christ: The righteous will live by faith becomes the banner text of the gospel: Paul quotes it in Romans 1:17 and Galatians 3:11 to prove that we are justified by faith in Christ alone, and Hebrews 10:38 uses it to urge perseverance. Jesus is both the righteous one who lived by perfect faith and the one who drank the cup of wrath so that the earth might be filled with the knowledge of God's glory in His face.
basic.god: We learn that God keeps His word on His own perfect schedule, that He is patient but never idle, that He hates greed, violence, exploitation, and idolatry, and that He is supremely committed to His own glory filling the whole earth. He is the living God in His holy temple before whom every proud mouth must finally fall silent.
basic.crossReferences: Romans 1:17, Galatians 3:11, and Hebrews 10:37 to 38 all take up 2:4. Isaiah 11:9 parallels the earth filled with the knowledge of the LORD. Revelation 14 and 16 pick up the cup of wrath and the fall of Babylon.
basic.oneStory: At the center of the book God reveals how His people survive judgment and how sinners are saved in every age: not by their own strength, which is doomed, but by faith. That single line runs straight into the New Testament gospel, and the promise of the earth filled with God's glory carries the story all the way to the new creation.

context.literary: God's answer has three parts: the command to write and wait, 2:2 to 3; the axis-verse contrasting the proud and the righteous, 2:4 to 5; and a taunt-song of five woes against the oppressor, 2:6 to 19, each opening with woe. The section is framed by two great refrains, the earth filled with God's glory, 2:14, and the LORD in His holy temple, 2:20.
context.historical: The woes catalog the sins of an imperial predator like Babylon: plunder and debt-slavery, building an empire by extortion, founding cities on bloodshed, degrading conquered peoples, and trusting lifeless idols. Ancient conquerors did loot the cedars of Lebanon and enslave whole populations, so the charges are pointed and concrete.
context.canonical: Habakkuk 2:4 is quoted three times in the New Testament and stands behind the Reformation recovery of justification by faith. The refrain of 2:14 echoes Isaiah 11:9 and points toward the consummation, while 2:20 supplies the church's call to reverent silence before God.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To deliver God's decisive answer: the vision is certain and timed, the proud will fall while the righteous live by faith, and the arrogant oppressor stands under fivefold woe, all so that God's people will trust and wait for the God whose glory will fill the earth.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The woes describe God's judgment on a specific oppressor, but they prescribe timeless warnings against greed, exploitation, violence, and idolatry. Verse 4 is prescriptive at the deepest level: the righteous, in every age, live by faith.

debates.question: In 2:4, does emunah mean faith or faithfulness, and how does the New Testament use it?

views.label: Faith as trust in God's promise
views.case: In context the righteous survive the crisis by trusting God rather than their own strength; the New Testament draws this out into justification by faith, quoting the verse in Romans 1:17 and Galatians 3:11.

views.label: Faithfulness as steadfast loyalty
views.case: The Hebrew word emphasizes steadfastness, so some render it the righteous will live by his faithfulness, stressing persevering loyalty; Hebrews 10:38 applies it to enduring and not shrinking back.

debates.bearing: The two are not opposed: saving faith is a trust that holds fast. The New Testament shows that this faith, not works, is how a sinner is justified and how the justified then persevere; both the initial trust and the enduring loyalty flow from the same reliance on God.

debates.question: Whose faith is in view, and is Paul reading it rightly?

views.label: The believer's faith
views.case: Habakkuk contrasts the self-reliant proud with the God-reliant righteous; Paul rightly universalizes this to every sinner justified by faith in Christ.

views.label: Primarily faithfulness within the covenant
views.case: Some argue the original stresses covenant loyalty more than the doctrine of justification, though they agree Paul develops a legitimate implication of the text.

debates.bearing: Historic Christian teaching, following Paul, reads the verse as establishing that righteousness is received and life is sustained by faith, the foundation of justification by faith alone.

study.originalLanguages: God commands Habakkuk to write the chazon, the vision, plainly, for it has an appointed time and will not fail. The centerpiece is the contrast in 2:4: the proud man's soul is not upright, puffed up and crooked, but the tsaddiq, the righteous one, will live by emunah. That word blends firm trust and steadfast faithfulness; it is a leaning-on-God that holds fast, and the New Testament unfolds it into justification by faith. The judgment section is a taunt-song, each stanza opening with hoy, woe, a funeral cry over the doomed oppressor. Two soaring lines interrupt the woes: the earth will be filled with the knowledge of the kavod, the glory, of the LORD; and the last woe exposes the elil, the worthless, breathless idol, over against the living LORD in His holy temple.

typology.type: The vision that will surely come and not delay
typology.fulfillment: The coming of Christ; Hebrews 10:37 applies the words to the one who is coming and will not delay, both His first advent and His return.

typology.type: The cup in the LORD's right hand passed to the oppressor
typology.fulfillment: The cup of wrath Jesus drinks in Gethsemane and at the cross for His people, and pours out on the impenitent at the end, Matthew 26:39, Revelation 14:10.

typology.type: The earth filled with the knowledge of God's glory
typology.fulfillment: The glory of God in the face of Christ now, 2 Corinthians 4:6, and the new creation filled with His glory, Revelation 21:23.

study.christ: The single line the righteous will live by faith becomes the seed of the New Testament gospel. Paul cites it in Romans 1:17 to announce that in the gospel the righteousness of God is revealed from faith to faith, and in Galatians 3:11 to prove that no one is justified before God by the law, for the righteous will live by faith. Hebrews 10:38 uses it to call believers to persevere and not shrink back. Jesus is the righteous One who lived by perfect faith and faithfulness, who drank the cup of wrath the oppressor deserved, and through whom the earth will be filled with the knowledge of the glory of God. The doctrine of justification by faith alone, recovered at the Reformation, rests squarely on this verse.
study.god: God is faithful to His word and sovereign over its timing: the vision has an appointed time and will not delay. He is patient yet never passive; He pronounces sure woe on greed, dishonest gain, bloodshed, exploitation, and idolatry. He is jealous for His own glory, purposing that it will cover the earth as waters cover the sea. And He is the living God enthroned in His holy temple, before whom every idol is exposed as breathless and every proud mouth must fall silent.

crossReferences.note: Paul quotes Habakkuk 2:4 as the theme of the gospel: the righteous will live by faith.
No one is justified by the law, for the righteous will live by faith; the charter of justification by faith.
Applies 2:3 to 4 to Christ's coming and to persevering faith that does not shrink back.
The earth will be full of the knowledge of the LORD as the waters cover the sea, parallel to Habakkuk 2:14.
The fall of Babylon the great, the fulfillment of the woes on the proud oppressor.

study.oneStory: At the very center of Habakkuk, God reveals the principle by which His people live in every age: not by their own doomed strength but by faith. That line runs straight into the gospel Paul preaches, becoming the foundation of justification by faith in Christ. The five woes assure us that every proud power will fall, and the twin refrains promise that God's glory will flood the earth and silence all opposition, carrying the whole story toward the new creation.

sermon.title: The Just Shall Live by Faith
sermon.why: Piper preaches directly on Habakkuk with 2:4 at the center, drawing out that the righteous live and are kept safe for eternity by faith in God, and connecting it to the gospel of justification by faith. A faithful, orthodox handling of the key verse of this movement.

quiz.q: What is the central statement of God's answer in this chapter?

options.text: The proud one is not upright, but the righteous will live by faith.
options.note: Habakkuk 2:4 is the hinge of the book and the seed of the New Testament gospel.

options.text: The proud will collapse under their arrogance while those who trust God endure.
options.note: A faithful paraphrase, though the exact wording of 2:4 is the key statement.

options.text: That the righteous will live by keeping the law perfectly.
options.note: The text says by faith, which the New Testament sets against justification by works.

options.text: That Babylon will rule forever unchallenged.
options.note: The opposite: Babylon stands under fivefold woe and will fall.

quiz.q: How does the New Testament use Habakkuk 2:4?

options.text: Paul and Hebrews quote it for justification by faith and persevering faith, Romans 1:17, Galatians 3:11, Hebrews 10:38.
options.note: It is one of the most cited Old Testament verses, foundational to the gospel.

options.text: It is used to show that righteousness is received through faith.
options.note: Correct in substance, but it names only part of how the New Testament deploys the verse.

options.text: It is quoted only once, by Jesus, about the temple.
options.note: It is quoted three times in the New Testament, by Paul and in Hebrews.

options.text: It is used to teach salvation by good works.
options.note: It teaches the opposite: the righteous live by faith, not works.

quiz.q: What do the five woes expose?

options.text: The self-destructive sins of the proud oppressor: greed, dishonest gain, bloodshed, cruelty, and idolatry.
options.note: Each woe unmasks a way the arrogant life ruins itself and others.

options.text: God's certain judgment coming on the arrogant and violent.
options.note: True, since a woe announces judgment, but the woes also name the specific sins condemned.

options.text: Minor ceremonial mistakes in worship.
options.note: They address grave moral evils, not small ritual errors.

options.text: God's own supposed injustice.
options.note: The woes are God's just verdict on human wickedness.

quiz.q: What is promised in the refrain of 2:14?

options.text: The earth will be filled with the knowledge of the glory of the LORD as the waters cover the sea.
options.note: The high point of the chapter: God's glory will flood the whole world.

options.text: That one day God's glory will be known across the whole earth.
options.note: A faithful paraphrase, though it drops the vivid image of waters covering the sea.

options.text: That the seas will dry up.
options.note: The waters covering the sea is an image of fullness, not of drought.

options.text: That Babylon's glory will spread over the earth.
options.note: It is the LORD's glory, not the oppressor's, that will fill the earth.

options.text: The LORD is in His holy temple; let all the earth be silent before Him.
options.note: A call to reverent silence before the living God, over against lifeless idols.

options.text: With a call to reverent awe before the sovereign, living God.
options.note: This captures the sense, though the actual words are the LORD in His holy temple, let all be silent.

options.text: With a promise that the proud will never be judged.
options.note: The whole section promises sure judgment on the proud.

options.text: With a command to build more idols of gold and silver.
options.note: The passage exposes idols as breathless and worthless.

quiz.q: What does living by faith mean in Habakkuk's own setting?

options.text: Trusting God and His promise to survive the crisis, rather than relying on one's own strength.
options.note: The righteous lean on God while they wait for the vision to come.

options.text: Waiting patiently for God to act instead of taking matters into one's own hands.
options.note: True and part of it, but the core is active trust in God and His promise.

options.text: Pretending the danger from Babylon does not exist.
options.note: Faith is not denial; it is trust in God amid real danger.

options.text: Earning God's favor through hard work.
options.note: It is trust in God, not achievement, that marks the righteous.

questions: God told Habakkuk to write the vision and wait, because it would surely come. Where are you being called to trust God's timing when His promise seems to linger?
The righteous will live by faith is the line the whole gospel hangs on. In plain terms, are you resting for your standing with God on your own performance or on faith in Christ?
The proud man's appetite was never satisfied. Where do you see the endless, restless hunger of pride or greed in your own heart, and how does trusting God free you from it?
The five woes show how sins like exploitation and idolatry destroy those who practice them. Is there any small idol or dishonest gain you are tolerating that God is calling you to abandon?
One day the earth will be filled with the knowledge of God's glory. How would living in the light of that certain future change your priorities this week?

translationNotes.note: The Septuagint and Hebrews 10:37 read the coming one personally, though he lingers, wait for him, since he will surely come; the Hebrew reads it of the vision. Either way the certainty and timing are the point.

The Hebrew emunah can be rendered faith or faithfulness; the BSB footnote notes the verse is cited in Romans 1:17, Galatians 3:11, and Hebrews 10:38. The original em dashes are rendered here as clean clause breaks.
The Dead Sea Scrolls read wealth where the Masoretic Text reads wine; the BSB follows the Scrolls. The sense is that ill-gotten gain betrays the arrogant man.
The Dead Sea Scrolls, Septuagint, and Syriac read and stagger where the Masoretic Text reads and expose your uncircumcision; both convey the shame poured out on the oppressor.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for Habakkuk 2:2-20. Poetic lines rendered as flowing prose within each verse. Original em dashes in verses 4 and 18 rendered as clean clause breaks; no em or en dashes used.
